results.label: MDR manufacturer obligations — Article 10
results.reliance: Can be used as a strong first draft
results.answer:
### **Core Obligations of a Medical Device Manufacturer under MDR 2017/745**

Under **MDR 2017/745**, a **manufacturer** (as defined in **Article 2(30)**) has the following **core obligations**, primarily outlined in **Article 10** and supplemented by other key provisions:

---

### **1. General Compliance & Device Safety (Article 10(1))**
- **Ensure conformity with MDR requirements** when placing devices on the market or putting them into service.
 > *"When placing their devices on the market or putting them into service, manufacturers shall ensure that they have been designed and manufactured in accordance with the requirements of this Regulation."* **[1] Article 10(1)**
- **Comply with General Safety and Performance Requirements (GSPR)** set out in **Annex I**, including risk management, clinical evaluation, and post-market surveillance.

---

### **2. Risk Management System (Article 10(2))**
- **Establish, document, implement, and maintain a risk management system** as described in **Section 3 of Annex I**.
 > **[1] Article 10(2)**
- **Continuously update risk management** throughout the device lifecycle, including post-market data.

---

### **3. Clinical Evaluation & Post-Market Clinical Follow-Up (PMCF) (Article 10(3))**
- **Conduct a clinical evaluation** in accordance with **Article 61** and **Annex XIV**, including:
 - Systematic collection and analysis of clinical data.
 - Demonstration of safety, performance, and clinical benefits.
 - **Post-Market Clinical Follow-Up (PMCF)** to continuously update clinical evidence.
 > *"Manufacturers shall conduct a clinical evaluation in accordance with the requirements set out in Article 61 and Annex XIV, including a PMCF."* **[1] Article 10(3)**

---

### **4. Technical Documentation (Article 10(4))**
- **Draw up and keep up-to-date technical documentation** for all devices (except custom-made devices).
 - Must allow assessment of conformity with MDR requirements.
 - Must include elements set out in **Annexes II and III** (e.g., device description, risk management, clinical evaluation, design and manufacturing information).
 > *"Manufacturers of devices other than custom-made devices shall draw up and keep up to date technical documentation for those devices. The technical documentation shall be such as to allow the conformity of the device with the requirements of this Regulation to be assessed. The technical documentation shall include the elements set out in Annexes II and III."* **[1] Article 10(4)**

---

### **5. Conformity Assessment & CE Marking (Article 10(6))**
- **Undertake conformity assessment** in accordance with **Article 52** and applicable **Annexes (IX–XI)**.
- **Draw up an EU Declaration of Conformity (Article 19)** and **affix the CE marking (Article 20)**.
 > *"Where compliance with the applicable requirements has been demonstrated following the applicable conformity assessment procedure, manufacturers of devices, other than custom-made or investigational devices, shall draw up an EU declaration of conformity in accordance with Article 19, and affix the CE marking of conformity in accordance with Article 20."* **[1] Article 10(6)**

---

### **6. UDI & Registration Obligations (Article 10(7))**
- **Comply with UDI system requirements (Article 27)**.
- **Register devices in EUDAMED (Article 29)** before placing them on the market.
 - Assign a **Basic UDI-DI** and provide core data elements to the UDI database.
 > **[1] Article 10(7)**

---

### **7. Post-Market Surveillance (PMS) & Vigilance (Article 10(10))**
- **Establish and maintain a PMS system (Article 83)** to:
 - Proactively collect and review post-market data.
 - Update risk management, clinical evaluation, and technical documentation.
 - Report **serious incidents (Article 87)** and **field safety corrective actions (FSCAs)**.
 > **[1] Article 10(10)**

---

### **8. Quality Management System (QMS) (Article 10(9))**
- **Establish, document, implement, and maintain a QMS** proportionate to the device’s risk class.
 - Must ensure compliance with MDR requirements.
 - Must include procedures for **risk management, clinical evaluation, PMS, and vigilance**.
 > **[1] Article 10(9)**

---

### **9. Corrective & Preventive Actions (Article 10(12))**
- **Take necessary corrective actions** (e.g., recalls, withdrawals) if a device is non-compliant or poses risks.
 - Inform **distributors, authorised representatives, and importers**.
 - Report **serious incidents (Article 87)** and **FSCAs** to competent authorities.
 > **[1] Article 10(12)**

---

### **10. Retention of Documentation (Article 10(8))**
- **Keep technical documentation, EU Declaration of Conformity, and certificates** for:
 - **10 years** after the last device is placed on the market (general rule).
 - **15 years** for **implantable devices**.
 > *"Manufacturers shall keep the technical documentation, the EU declaration of conformity and, if applicable, a copy of any relevant certificate, including any amendments and supplements, issued in accordance with Article 56, available for the competent authorities for a period of at least 10 years after the last device covered by the EU declaration of conformity has been placed on the market. In the case of implantable devices, the period shall be at least 15 years after the last device has been placed on the market."* **[1] Article 10(8)**

---

### **11. Person Responsible for Regulatory Compliance (PRRC) (Article 15)**
- **Have at least one PRRC** within the organisation (or permanently available for micro/small enterprises).
 - PRRC must ensure:
 - Conformity checks before device release.
 - Technical documentation and EU Declaration of Conformity are up to date.
 - PMS and vigilance obligations are fulfilled.
 - Reporting obligations (Articles 87–91) are met.
 > *"Manufacturers shall have available within their organisation at least one person responsible for regulatory compliance who possesses the requisite expertise in the field of medical devices."* **[2] Article 15(1)**

---

### **12. Supply Chain & Economic Operator Obligations (Articles 10(15), 16)**
- **Identify suppliers** (if devices are designed/manufactured by third parties) and include their details in EUDAMED.
 > *"Where manufacturers have their devices designed or manufactured by another legal or natural person the information on the identity of that person shall be part of the information to be submitted in accordance with Article 29(4)."* **[1] Article 10(15)**
- **Monitor distributors/importers** to ensure they do not modify devices in a way that affects compliance (Article 16).

---

### **13. Interruption/Discontinuation of Supply (Article 10a)**
- **Notify competent authorities, economic operators, and healthcare professionals** if an interruption/discontinuation of supply could result in **serious harm or risk to public health**.
 > *"Where a manufacturer anticipates an interruption or a discontinuation of the supply of a device, other than a custom-made device, and where it is reasonably foreseeable that such interruption or discontinuation could result in serious harm or a risk of serious harm to patients or public health in one or more Member States, the manufacturer shall inform the competent authority..."* **[7] Article 10a(1)**

---

### **Summary of Key Obligations**
| **Obligation** | **Key Requirement** | **Relevant Article(s)** |
|---------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| **General Compliance** | Ensure devices meet MDR requirements | **Article 10(1)** |
| **Risk Management** | Establish and maintain a risk management system | **Article 10(2), Annex I Section 3** |
| **Clinical Evaluation** | Conduct clinical evaluation and PMCF | **Article 10(3), 61, Annex XIV** |
| **Technical Documentation** | Prepare and update technical documentation | **Article 10(4), Annexes II & III** |
| **Conformity Assessment** | Undertake conformity assessment and affix CE marking | **Article 10(6), 52, 19, 20** |
| **UDI & Registration** | Assign UDI and register in EUDAMED | **Article 10(7), 27, 29** |
| **Post-Market Surveillance** | Establish PMS system and report incidents | **Article 10(10), 83, 87** |
| **Quality Management System** | Implement and maintain a QMS | **Article 10(9)** |
| **Corrective Actions** | Take corrective actions (recall, withdrawal) if needed | **Article 10(12)** |
| **Document Retention** | Keep documentation for 10/15 years | **Article 10(8)** |
| **PRRC** | Appoint a Person Responsible for Regulatory Compliance | **Article 15** |
| **Supply Chain Monitoring** | Ensure suppliers/distributors comply | **Article 10(15), 16** |
| **Supply Interruption** | Notify authorities if supply disruption poses serious risk | **Article 10a** |

---

### **Conclusion**
The **MDR 2017/745** imposes **comprehensive obligations** on manufacturers, covering **pre-market compliance, post-market surveillance, risk management, clinical evaluation, and vigilance**. Compliance requires **continuous monitoring, documentation, and proactive risk mitigation** throughout the device lifecycle. Failure to meet these obligations can result in **market withdrawal, recalls, or regulatory sanctions**.

> ⚠ **ATTRIBUTION FLAG** — 5 of 13 quote(s) contain real regulatory text that does not belong to the provision they cite (or concatenate text from several provisions under one citation) and have been removed. Re-check which provision each obligation actually comes from.
> - “Manufacturers shall establish, document, implement and maintain a system for risk management as described in Section...”
> - “Manufacturers shall comply with the obligations relating to the UDI system referred to in Article 27 and with the reg...”
> - “Manufacturers shall implement and keep up to date the post-market surveillance system in accordance with Article 83.”
> - “Manufacturers of devices, other than investigational devices, shall establish, document, implement, maintain, keep up...”
> - “Manufacturers who consider or have reason to believe that a device which they have placed on the market or put into s...”

results.label: MDR Class IIb conformity assessment route (Annex IX vs Annex X)
results.reliance: Can be used as a strong first draft
results.answer:
### **Direct Answer**
For a **Class IIb medical device** under **MDR 2017/745**, the **conformity assessment route** is defined in **Article 52(4)**. The manufacturer must follow the procedure set out in **Chapters I and III of Annex IX**, which includes:

1. **Conformity assessment based on a quality management system (QMS)** (Annex IX, Chapters I and III), **plus**
2. **Assessment of the technical documentation** for **at least one representative device per generic device group** (Annex IX, Section 4).

Alternatively, the manufacturer **may not** use Annex X (type-examination) + Annex XI (production quality assurance) for Class IIb devices—this route is **only available for Class III devices** (Article 52(3)).

The **notified body’s role** is to:
- **Audit the manufacturer’s QMS** (Annex IX, Section 2.3–2.4),
- **Assess the technical documentation** of the representative device(s) (Annex IX, Section 4),
- **Issue a certificate** confirming conformity (Annex IX, Section 2.5),
- **Conduct surveillance** to ensure ongoing compliance (Annex IX, Section 3).

---

### **Regulatory Grounding**

#### **1. Conformity Assessment Route for Class IIb Devices[2] Article 52(4) [BINDING]**
> *"Manufacturers of class IIb devices, other than custom-made or investigational devices, shall be subject to a conformity assessment as specified in Chapters I and III of Annex IX, and including an assessment of the technical documentation as specified in Section 4 of that Annex of at least one representative device per generic device group."*

- **Key requirement**: The notified body must assess **technical documentation for at least one representative device per generic device group** (Annex IX, Section 4).
- **No alternative route**: Unlike Class III devices (which may use Annex X + XI), Class IIb devices **must** use Annex IX.

#### **2. Role of the Notified Body**
The notified body’s obligations are detailed in **Annex IX**:

**[2] Annex IX (Conformity Assessment Based on QMS + Technical Documentation) [BINDING]**
- **Section 2.3–2.4 (QMS Audit)**:
 > 
 - The notified body **verifies the manufacturer’s QMS** (e.g., ISO 13485 compliance).

- **Section 4 (Technical Documentation Assessment)**:
 > 
 - The notified body **reviews the technical file** (Annex II + III) for **at least one representative device per generic group**.

- **Section 2.5 (Certificate Issuance)**:
 > 
 - The certificate confirms that the **QMS and technical documentation** meet MDR requirements.

- **Section 3 (Surveillance)**:
 > 
 - The notified body **conducts ongoing surveillance** (e.g., annual audits) to ensure continued compliance.

---

### **Key Takeaways**
1. **Mandatory Route**: Class IIb devices **must** use **Annex IX (QMS + technical documentation assessment)**.
2. **Notified Body’s Role**:
 - **Audits the QMS** (Annex IX, Section 2).
 - **Assesses technical documentation** for at least one representative device per generic group (Annex IX, Section 4).
 - **Issues a certificate** (Annex IX, Section 2.5).
 - **Conducts surveillance** (Annex IX, Section 3).
3. **No Alternative**: Annex X (type-examination) + XI (production QA) is **not available** for Class IIb devices (only for Class III).

---

### **Cross-References**
- **Article 52(3)** (Class III alternative routes) → Not applicable to Class IIb.
- **Annex II** (Technical Documentation) → Must be assessed by the notified body.
- **Annex IX, Section 4** → Requires assessment of **representative devices**.
- **Article 10(9)** → Manufacturer must have a **QMS** (audited by the notified body).

> ⚠ **FAITHFULNESS FLAG** — 4 of 5 verbatim quote(s) could not be matched against the retrieved regulatory context and have been removed from the answer. Treat the underlying point as unverified until checked against the source provisions.
> - “The notified body shall audit the quality management system to determine whether it meets the requirements referred t...”
> - “The notified body shall assess the technical documentation for the device(s) selected on a representative basis to ve...”
> - “The notified body shall issue a certificate of conformity relating to the quality management system.”
> - “The notified body shall periodically carry out appropriate audits and assessments to make sure that the manufacturer...”

results.label: IVDR classification — Class B vs C (notified body involvement)
results.reliance: Can be used as a strong first draft
results.answer:
### **Direct Answer**
Under **IVDR 2017/746**, an **in vitro diagnostic (IVD)** is classified as **Class B** or **Class C** based on **Annex VIII (Classification Rules)**, which assesses the **intended purpose** and **inherent risk** of the device. The key distinction lies in the **severity of the condition** the IVD detects and the **consequences of an incorrect result**.

- **Class B** applies to **low-to-moderate risk** IVDs (e.g., general laboratory tests, self-tests for non-serious conditions).
- **Class C** applies to **moderate-to-high risk** IVDs (e.g., tests for serious infectious diseases, cancer markers, genetic testing).

A **notified body (NB) must be involved** for **Class C (and Class D) IVDs** as part of the **conformity assessment procedure** (Article 48). **Class B IVDs** may be self-certified by the manufacturer, except when they are **sterile** (then NB involvement is required).

---

### **1. Classification Rules (Annex VIII) – Class B vs. Class C**
The **IVDR classification system** (Annex VIII) is **risk-based**, with **Class A (lowest risk) to Class D (highest risk)**. The classification depends on:

#### **Key Rules for Class B vs. Class C**
| **Rule** | **Class B** | **Class C** | **Examples** |
|----------|------------|------------|--------------|
| **Rule 1** | Not applicable (Class D for life-threatening transmissible agents) | **Detection of serious infectious diseases** (e.g., HIV, hepatitis, tuberculosis) | HIV antibody test (Class C) |
| **Rule 2** | **Blood grouping (non-critical markers)** (e.g., ABO, Rh) | **Blood grouping for critical markers** (e.g., Kell, Duffy) or **tissue typing for transplantation** | ABO blood typing (Class B) vs. HLA typing (Class C) |
| **Rule 3** | **Self-tests for non-serious conditions** (e.g., pregnancy, glucose monitoring) | **Self-tests for serious conditions** (e.g., cancer markers, genetic predisposition) | Pregnancy test (Class B) vs. BRCA genetic test (Class C) |
| **Rule 4** | **General laboratory tests** (e.g., routine biochemistry, hematology) | **Tests for serious diseases** (e.g., cancer, cardiovascular risk) | Cholesterol test (Class B) vs. PSA test (Class C) |
| **Rule 5** | **Devices for general use** (e.g., culture media, specimen receptacles) | **Devices for specific high-risk purposes** (e.g., microbiological culture for serious pathogens) | Sterile specimen container (Class B) vs. culture media for MRSA (Class C) |
| **Rule 6** | **Companion diagnostics for non-critical drugs** | **Companion diagnostics for critical drugs** (e.g., cancer therapies) | Companion diagnostic for a non-life-threatening drug (Class B) vs. for a cancer drug (Class C) |

#### **Key Differences in Risk Level**
- **Class B**: **Low-to-moderate risk** – Incorrect results may lead to **minor health issues** or **non-critical decisions**.
- **Class C**: **Moderate-to-high risk** – Incorrect results may lead to **serious health consequences**, **misdiagnosis of life-threatening conditions**, or **inappropriate treatment**.

#### **Implementing Rules (Annex VIII, Section 1)**
- **Rule 1.8**: If multiple intended purposes apply, the **highest class** prevails.
- **Rule 1.9**: If multiple classification rules apply, the **highest class** prevails.
- **Rule 1.4**: **Software** driving or influencing an IVD is classified **the same as the device** it controls.

---

### **2. When is a Notified Body Required? (Article 48)**
The **conformity assessment procedure** (Article 48) determines whether a **notified body (NB)** must be involved:

| **Class** | **Notified Body Involvement?** | **Conformity Assessment Procedure** |
|-----------|-------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| **Class A (non-sterile)** | ❌ No | **Self-certification** (Annex III) |
| **Class A (sterile)** | ✅ Yes | **Annex IX (QMS) + Annex XI (Batch Testing)** |
| **Class B** | ❌ No (unless sterile) | **Self-certification (Annex III)** |
| **Class B (sterile)** | ✅ Yes | **Annex IX (QMS) + Annex XI (Batch Testing)** |
| **Class C** | ✅ Yes | **Annex IX (QMS + Technical Documentation Review)** or **Annex X (Type Examination) + Annex XI (Batch Testing)** |
| **Class D** | ✅ Yes (highest scrutiny) | **Annex IX (QMS + EU Reference Lab Testing)** or **Annex X + Annex XI** |

#### **Key Provisions (Article 48)**
- **Class C IVDs** require **NB assessment** before placing on the market (Article 48(7)).
- **Class B IVDs** (non-sterile) can be **self-certified** (Article 48(10)).
- **Sterile Class B IVDs** require **NB involvement** (Article 48(9), second subparagraph).

---

### **3. Legal Basis for Classification & NB Involvement**
#### **Classification (Annex VIII)**
- **Article 47(1)**: Devices are classified into **Class A, B, C, or D** based on **Annex VIII**.
- **Annex VIII, Rule 1.1**: Classification is **governed by intended purpose**.
- **Annex VIII, Rule 1.8 & 1.9**: If multiple rules apply, the **highest class** prevails.

#### **Notified Body Involvement (Article 48)**
- **Article 48(1)**: Manufacturers must **assess conformity** before placing a device on the market.
- **Article 48(7)**: **Class C IVDs** require **NB assessment** (Annex IX or X + XI).
- **Article 48(9)**: **Class B IVDs** (non-sterile) are **self-certified** (Annex III).
- **Article 48(9), second subparagraph**: **Sterile Class B IVDs** require **NB involvement** (Annex IX + XI).

---

### **4. Practical Implications**
#### **For Class B IVDs (Non-Sterile)**
- **No NB involvement** (self-certification).
- **Technical documentation** (Annex II & III) must be prepared.
- **EU Declaration of Conformity (Article 17)** must be issued.
- **UDI registration (Article 26)** is required.

#### **For Class C IVDs**
- **NB must be involved** (Annex IX or X + XI).
- **Performance evaluation (Article 56)** must demonstrate **scientific validity, analytical performance, and clinical performance**.
- **Post-market surveillance (Article 78)** is mandatory.
- **UDI registration (Article 26)** is required before applying to the NB.

---

### **5. Summary of Key Differences**
| **Aspect** | **Class B IVD** | **Class C IVD** |
|------------|----------------|----------------|
| **Risk Level** | Low-to-moderate | Moderate-to-high |
| **Examples** | Pregnancy test, cholesterol test, ABO blood typing | HIV test, cancer markers, genetic testing, HLA typing |
| **Notified Body Required?** | ❌ No (unless sterile) | ✅ Yes |
| **Conformity Assessment** | Self-certification (Annex III) | Annex IX or X + XI |
| **Performance Evaluation** | Required (Article 56) | Required (Article 56) + stricter scrutiny |
| **Post-Market Surveillance** | Required (Article 78) | Required (Article 78) + more stringent reporting |

---

### **Conclusion**
- **Class B vs. Class C** is determined by **Annex VIII classification rules**, focusing on **intended purpose and risk**.
- **Notified body involvement is mandatory for Class C IVDs** (Article 48(7)) but **not for non-sterile Class B IVDs** (Article 48(10)).
- **Sterile Class B IVDs** still require **NB assessment** (Article 48(9), second subparagraph).

Would you like a **specific example** (e.g., a glucose monitor vs. an HIV test) to illustrate the classification?

Article 5(3) applies to **conformity assessment procedures for CE-marked devices**, not the Article 5(5) exemption. The correct legal anchor for clinical evaluation under Article 5(5) is **Annex I (GSPR), Point 1**, which mandates clinical evaluation as part of the GSPR compliance process.

### **Direct Answer**
Under **MDR Article 120(3c)(b)**, a **"significant change in the design or intended purpose"** of a legacy device (CE-marked under the MDD/AIMDD) **terminates the transitional benefit** and requires full MDR certification before the device can remain on the market. The assessment is **case-by-case**, but the following **general principles** apply based on the regulatory context:

---

### **1. Legal Definition & Two-Part Test**
MDR **Article 120(3c)(b)** [BINDING] states that legacy devices may remain on the market **only if** they do **not** undergo a **"significant change in the design or intended purpose"** after 26 May 2021.

The **MDCG 2020-3 Rev.1 guidance** [NON-BINDING] clarifies that a "significant changesignificantsignificantChange in Design or Intended Purposedesign,The use for which the device is intended according to the data supplied by the manufacturer on the labelling, in the instructions for use and/or in promotional materials.SignificantsignificantDoes the change affect safety or performance and negatively affect the benefit/risk ratio?significant change," the change **terminates the transitional benefit**.

**→ This guidance is non-binding but is the primary operational tool for manufacturers.**

---

### **Conclusion: Likely Outcomes for Common Scenarios**
| **Scenario** | **Likely Outcome** | **Conditions for Opposite Outcome** |
|------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Bug fix in software** | **Non-significant** (if no new risks). | If the bug affects safety/performance (e.g., incorrect dosing algorithm). |
| **New language in IFU** | **Non-significant** (editorial change). | If the new language introduces a new medical claim. |
| **Change of material supplier** | **Non-significant** (if within specs). | If the new material has different biocompatibility or durability. |
| **Extension of intended purpose (e.g., new medical indication)** | **Significant** (always). | N/A (extensions are per se significant). |
| **Change of sterilisation method** | **Significant** (unless validated as non-impactful). | If the new method is **equivalent** in sterility assurance. |

| **Corrective action approved by competent authority** | **Non-significant** (per MDCG 2020-3 Rev.1). | If the authority later revokes approval. |

**Final Note**: If in doubt, **consult the notified body**—their written confirmation can provide legal certainty.

results.label: MDR post-market surveillance and PMCF (Articles 83-86)
results.reliance: Can be used as a strong first draft
results.answer:
### **1. Post-Market Surveillance (PMS) Obligations**
Under **MDR 2017/745**, manufacturers must establish, implement, and maintain a **proportionate and risk-class-appropriate** post-market surveillance system as an integral part of their **quality management system (QMS)** (Article 10(9) and Article 83(1)).

#### **Key Obligations:**
**(a) Establish a PMS System (Article 83)**
- The system must **actively and systematically** gather, record, and analyse **relevant data** on the **quality, performance, and safety** of the device **throughout its entire lifetime**.
- It must enable the manufacturer to:
  - Update the **benefit-risk determination** and improve **risk management** (Annex I, Chapter I).
  - Update **design, manufacturing information, instructions for use (IFU), and labelling** (Article 83(3)(b)).
  - Update the **clinical evaluation** (Article 83(3)(c)).
  - Update the **Summary of Safety and Clinical Performance (SSCP)** (Article 32) for **implantable and Class III devices**.
  - Identify needs for **preventive, corrective, or field safety corrective actions (FSCA)** (Article 83(3)(e)).
  - Improve **usability, performance, and safety** (Article 83(3)(f)).
  - Contribute to the **PMS of other devices** (Article 83(3)(g)).
  - **Detect and report trends** (Article 88).

**(b) Develop a PMS Plan (Article 84 & Annex III, Section 1)**
- The PMS system must be **based on a PMS plan**, which is part of the **technical documentation** (Annex II) for **non-custom-made devices**.
- The plan must address:
  - **Data collection methods** (e.g., complaints, literature, post-market clinical follow-up (PMCF), vigilance reports).
  - **Data analysis** (e.g., trend analysis, risk assessment).
  - **Reporting mechanisms** (e.g., vigilance, FSCA, trend reporting).
  - **Roles and responsibilities** within the manufacturer’s organisation.

**(c) Prepare PMS Reports (Article 85 & Article 86)**
- **Class I devices**: Manufacturers must prepare a **PMS report** summarising results, conclusions, and any **preventive/corrective actions** taken. This report must be **updated as necessary** and made available to **competent authorities upon request** (Article 85).
- **Class IIa, IIb, and III devices**: Manufacturers must prepare a **Periodic Safety Update Report (PSUR)** (Article 86), which:
  - Summarises **PMS data**, **vigilance reports**, and **trend analyses**.
  - Includes **conclusions on benefit-risk determination** and **corrective/preventive actions**.
  - Is **updated at least annually** for **Class III and implantable devices** and **every two years** for **Class IIa and IIb devices** (unless otherwise specified by the notified body).
  - Must be **submitted to the notified body** (for Class IIa, IIb, and III devices) and **made available to competent authorities** via **Eudamed** (Article 92).

**(d) Vigilance Reporting (Article 87)**
- Manufacturers must **report serious incidents** and **FSCAs** to **competent authorities** via **Eudamed** (Article 92).
  - **Serious incidents** must be reported **immediately** (but no later than **15 days** after awareness), or **within 10 days** for incidents involving **death or unanticipated serious deterioration in health**, or **within 2 days** for **serious public health threats** (Article 87(3)-(5)).
  - **FSCAs** must be reported **in advance** (unless urgent action is required) (Article 87(8)).
- **Trend reporting** is required for **statistically significant increases** in the frequency/severity of **non-serious incidents** or **expected undesirable side-effects** that could impact the **benefit-risk analysis** (Article 88).

---

### **2. Post-Market Clinical Follow-Up (PMCF) Obligations**
PMCF is a **continuous process** to **update the clinical evaluation** (Article 61 and Annex XIV) and is **mandatory for all devices** unless duly justified otherwise.

#### **Key Obligations:**
**(a) Conduct PMCF as Part of Clinical Evaluation (Annex XIV, Part B)**
- PMCF must be **planned, conducted, and documented** to:
  - **Confirm safety and performance** throughout the device’s expected lifetime.
  - **Identify unknown side-effects** and monitor **known side-effects**.
  - **Ensure continued acceptability of the benefit-risk ratio**.
  - **Identify possible systematic misuse or off-label use**.
- The **PMCF plan** must be **proportionate to the device’s risk class** and **intended purpose** and may include:
  - **Collection and analysis of clinical data** from device use (e.g., registries, post-market studies, literature).
  - **Feedback from users, patients, and healthcare professionals**.
  - **Post-market clinical investigations** (if necessary to address gaps in clinical evidence).

**(b) Update Clinical Evaluation and Technical Documentation (Annex XIV & Annex II)**
- PMCF data must be used to **update the clinical evaluation report (CER)** and **technical documentation** (Annex II).
- The **CER must be updated at least annually** for **Class III and implantable devices** and **when new clinical data becomes available** (Annex XIV, Part A, Section 1a).

**(c) Include PMCF in the PMS System (Article 83(3)(c))**
- PMCF is a **key component of PMS** and must feed into the **PMS plan, PMS report (Class I), and PSUR (Class IIa-III)**.
- PMCF findings must be used to **update the SSCP** (for implantable and Class III devices) (Article 32).

---

### **3. Interaction Between PMS and PMCF**
| **Aspect**               | **Post-Market Surveillance (PMS)**                          | **Post-Market Clinical Follow-Up (PMCF)**                  |
|--------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------|
| **Purpose**              | Proactively monitor **safety, performance, and quality**   | Proactively gather **clinical data** to confirm **safety and performance** |
| **Scope**                | Broader (includes **vigilance, complaints, literature**)   | Focused on **clinical data** (e.g., studies, registries)  |
| **Legal Basis**          | Article 83–86, Annex III                                   | Article 61, Annex XIV                                     |
| **Outputs**              | PMS report (Class I), PSUR (Class IIa-III), vigilance reports | Updated **clinical evaluation report (CER)**, PMCF plan, PMCF evaluation report |
| **Frequency**            | Continuous; reports updated as needed                       | Continuous; CER updated at least **annually (Class III/implantable)** |
| **Submission**           | PMS report (on request), PSUR (to notified body)           | PMCF data integrated into **CER and PSUR**                |

---

### **4. Key Takeaways for Manufacturers**
1. **PMS is mandatory for all devices** and must be **proportionate to risk class**.
2. **PMCF is mandatory unless justified otherwise** and must be **documented in the clinical evaluation**.
3. **PMS and PMCF are interconnected**:
   - PMCF data feeds into **PMS reports (Class I) and PSURs (Class IIa-III)**.
   - PMS data (e.g., vigilance reports) may trigger **additional PMCF activities**.
4. **Reporting obligations**:
   - **Serious incidents and FSCAs** must be reported via **Eudamed** (Article 87, Article 92).
   - **Trend reporting** is required for **non-serious incidents** (Article 88).
5. **Documentation updates**:
   - **Technical documentation** (Annex II) must be updated with **PMS and PMCF findings**.
   - **Clinical evaluation report (CER)** must be updated **at least annually for Class III/implantable devices**.
   - **SSCP** (for implantable and Class III devices) must be updated with **PMS/PMCF data** (Article 32).

---

### **5. Non-Compliance Consequences**
Failure to comply with **PMS and PMCF obligations** may result in:
- **Corrective actions** by **competent authorities** (Article 95).
- **Withdrawal or recall** of the device from the market.
- **Suspension or revocation of CE certificates** by notified bodies.
- **Legal liability** for damages caused by non-compliant devices.

results.label: MDR importer vs distributor obligations (Article 13 vs 14)
results.reliance: Can be used as a strong first draft
results.answer:
### **Direct Answer**
Under **MDR 2017/745**, **importers** and **distributors** share core obligations (e.g., traceability, complaint handling, cooperation with authorities), but **importers** have **additional pre-market verification duties** and **direct registration obligations** because they place devices from third countries onto the EU market. Distributors focus on **post-market checks** and **supply chain traceability** for devices already on the EU market.

---

### **Key Differences in Obligations**

#### **1. Pre-Market Verification (Importer-Specific)Importers** must verify **before placing a device on the market** that:
- The device is **CE marked** and the **EU declaration of conformity** is drawn up ([3] Article 13(2)(a)).
- A **manufacturer is identified**, and an **authorised representative** (if the manufacturer is outside the EU) is designated ([3] Article 13(2)(b)).
- The device is **labelled in accordance with MDR** and accompanied by **required instructions for use** ([3] Article 13(2)(c)).
- A **UDI has been assigned** by the manufacturer ([3] Article 13(2)(d)).
- The device is **registered in Eudamed** (via Article 29), and the importer **adds its details** to the registration ([3] Article 13(4)).

**Distributors** only verify **before making a device available on the market** that:
- The device is **CE marked** and the **EU declaration of conformity** exists ([1] Article 14(2)(a)).
- The device is accompanied by **required information** (e.g., instructions for use) ([1] Article 14(2)(b)).
- For **imported devices**, the importer has complied with its obligations ([1] Article 14(2)(c)).
- A **UDI is assigned** (if applicable) ([1] Article 14(2)(d)).

**Legal Basis**:
- Importer: [3] Article 13(2) (pre-market verification).
- Distributor: [1] Article 14(2) (post-market checks).

---

#### **2. Registration Obligations (Importer-Specific)**
- **Importers** must **register themselves in Eudamed** before placing a device on the market ([6] Article 31(1)) and **add their details** to the device registration ([3] Article 13(4)).
- **Distributors** have **no registration obligation** under MDR (though Member States may impose national rules per [4] Article 30(2)).

**Legal Basis**:
- Importer: [6] Article 31(1) + [3] Article 13(4).
- Distributor: No MDR obligation (national rules possible per [4] Article 30(2)).

---

#### **3. Labelling Requirements (Importer-Specific)**
- **Importers** must **indicate their name, registered trade name/trademark, and address** on the device, packaging, or accompanying document ([3] Article 13(3)).
- **Distributors** only add their details if they **repackage/relabel** the device (e.g., for translation or pack size changes) ([2] Article 16(3)).

**Legal Basis**:
- Importer: [3] Article 13(3).
- Distributor: [2] Article 16(3) (only if modifying packaging/label).

---

#### **4. Post-Market Surveillance (Shared but Importer-Specific Triggers)**
Both must:
- **Monitor complaints/incidents** and forward them to the manufacturer ([1] Article 14(5) for distributors; [3] Article 13(8) for importers).
- **Keep a register of complaints, non-conforming devices, and recalls** ([1] Article 14(5); [3] Article 13(6)).
- **Cooperate with authorities** on corrective actions ([1] Article 14(4); [3] Article 13(7)).

**Importer-specific**:
- If an importer **considers a device non-compliant**, it must **immediately inform the manufacturer and competent authorities** ([3] Article 13(7)).
- If the device presents a **serious risk**, the importer must **inform competent authorities and the notified body** (if applicable) ([3] Article 13(7)).

**Legal Basis**:
- Shared: [1] Article 14(4)-(5) (distributors); [3] Article 13(6)-(8) (importers).
- Importer-specific: [3] Article 13(7) (serious risk reporting).

---

#### **5. Traceability (Shared)**
Both must **identify supply chain actors** (e.g., who supplied them, to whom they supplied the device) for **10–15 years** ([5] Article 25(2)).

**Legal Basis**:
- [5] Article 25(2) (applies to both).

---

#### **6. Manufacturer-Like Obligations (Triggered for Distributors)**
A distributor **assumes manufacturer obligations** if it:
- **Rebrands the device** (makes it available under its own name/trademark) ([2] Article 16(1)(a)).
- **Changes the intended purpose** of the device ([2] Article 16(1)(b)).
- **Modifies the device** in a way that may affect compliance ([2] Article 16(1)(c)).

**Exceptions**:
- **Translating instructions for use** or **repackaging** (if sterile conditions are maintained) **do not trigger manufacturer obligations** ([2] Article 16(2)).

**Legal Basis**:
- [2] Article 16(1)-(2).

---

### **Summary Table**
| **Obligation** | **Importer** | **Distributor** |
|------------------------------|---------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Pre-market verification** | ✔ (CE mark, manufacturer, UDI, etc.) | ✔ (CE mark, instructions, UDI) |
| **Registration in Eudamed** | ✔ (self + device details) | ❌ (unless national rules apply) |
| **Labelling requirements** | ✔ (name/address on device) | ❌ (only if repackaging/relabelling) |
| **Post-market surveillance** | ✔ (complaints, serious risk reporting)| ✔ (complaints, cooperation) |
| **Traceability** | ✔ (supply chain records) | ✔ (supply chain records) |
| **Manufacturer obligations** | ❌ (unless modifying device) | ✔ (if rebranding/changing purpose) |

---

### **Key Takeaways**
1. **Importers act as a "gatekeepermanufacturers"** if they modify the device or its intended purpose, triggering full MDR obligations.

> - “** for devices entering the EU market, with **stricter pre-market checks** and **registration duties**. 2. **Distribu...”

results.label: MDR authorised representative mandate for a non-EU manufacturer (Article 11)
results.reliance: Can be used as a strong first draft
results.answer:
### **Direct Answer**
Under **MDR Article 11**, the **authorised representative** (AR) is a **legally mandated intermediary** for non-EU manufacturers placing devices on the EU market. Its **core role** is to act as the manufacturer’s **EU-based point of contact** for regulatory compliance, but it **cannot delegate the manufacturer’s substantive obligations** (e.g., design, clinical evaluation, risk management). The AR’s **key obligations** are:

1. **Accept and hold a written mandate** covering at least all devices of the same **generic device group** (Article 11(2)).
2. **Perform the tasks specified in the mandate**, which typically include:
 - Verifying the manufacturer’s **EU declaration of conformity** and **technical documentation** (including risk management, clinical evaluation, and post-market surveillance plans).
 - Keeping a **copy of the technical documentation** and **EU declaration of conformity** available for competent authorities for **10–15 years** (depending on device class).
 - **Forwarding requests** from competent authorities to the manufacturer and ensuring the manufacturer provides the requested information.
 - **Cooperating with authorities** on preventive or corrective actions (e.g., field safety corrective actions, recalls).
 - **Terminating the mandate** if the manufacturer fails to comply with MDR obligations and **immediately informing** the competent authority and notified body (Article 11(6)).
3. **Joint and several liability** for defective devices if the manufacturer fails to comply with **Article 10 obligations** (e.g., risk management, clinical evaluation, post-market surveillance) (Article 11(5)).

---

### **Regulatory Grounding**

#### **1. Legal Definition and Mandate Scope**
- **Formal definition** ([1] Article 2 MDR):
 > ‘authorised representative’ means any natural or legal person established within the Union who has received and accepted a **written mandate** from a manufacturer, located outside the Union, to act on the manufacturer's behalf in relation to specified tasks with regard to the latter's obligations under this Regulation.

- **Mandate requirements** ([2] Article 11(2) MDR):
 > The designation shall constitute the authorised representative's mandate, it shall be **valid only when accepted in writing** by the authorised representative and shall be **effective at least for all devices of the same generic device group**.

#### **2. Tasks Specified in the Mandate**
- **Article 11(3) MDR**:
 > The authorised representative shall **perform the tasks specified in the mandate** agreed between it and the manufacturer. The authorised representative shall **provide a copy of the mandate to the competent authority, upon request**.

 - **Typical tasks** (non-exhaustive, based on MDR obligations):
 - Verifying the manufacturer’s **EU declaration of conformity** (Article 19).
 - Ensuring the manufacturer has **drafted and updated technical documentation** (Annexes II–III, Article 10(4)).
 - **Cooperating with competent authorities** on vigilance reporting (Articles 87–89).
 - **Forwarding requests** for information from authorities to the manufacturer (Article 10(14)).

#### **3. Non-Delegable Manufacturer Obligations**
- **Article 11(4) MDR**:
 > The mandate referred to in paragraph 3 of this Article **shall not delegate the manufacturer's obligations** laid down in Article 10(1), (2), (3), (4), (6), (7), (9), (10), (11) and (12).

 - **Key non-delegable obligations** (Article 10 MDR):
 - **Design and manufacture** in accordance with MDR requirements (Article 10(1)).
 - **Risk management system** (Article 10(2), Annex I Section 3).
 - **Clinical evaluation** (Article 10(3), Article 61).
 - **Technical documentation** (Article 10(4), Annexes II–III).
 - **Post-market surveillance system** (Article 10(10), Article 83).

#### **4. Liability for Non-Compliance**
- **Article 11(5) MDR**:
 > Where the manufacturer is not established in a Member State and has not complied with the obligations laid down in Article 10, the authorised representative shall be **legally liable for defective devices on the same basis as, and jointly and severally with, the manufacturer**.

 - **Scope of liability**: Covers **defective devices** resulting from the manufacturer’s failure to comply with **Article 10 obligations** (e.g., inadequate risk management, clinical evaluation, or post-market surveillance).

#### **5. Termination of Mandate**
- **Article 11(6) MDR**:
 > An authorised representative who terminates its mandate on the ground referred to in point (h) of paragraph 3 shall **immediately inform the competent authority** of the Member State in which it is established and, where applicable, the notified body that was involved in the conformity assessment for the device of the **termination of the mandate and the reasons therefor**.

 - **Point (h) of paragraph 3** (implied): Termination due to the manufacturer’s **non-compliance with MDR obligations**.

---

### **Key Legal Inferences**
1. **AR as a "Gatekeeperrepresentative"** (Article 4(17) GDPR), which is required for **non-EU controllers/processors** of personal data. The two roles **may be held by the same entity**, but their obligations differ:
 - **MDR AR**: Focuses on **device safety and compliance**.
 - **GDPR Representative**: Focuses on **data protection compliance** (e.g., responding to data subject requests, cooperating with supervisory authorities).

---

### **Conclusion**
The **authorised representative under MDR Article 11** is a **critical compliance intermediary** for non-EU manufacturers, with **verificatory, administrative, and liability obligations**. However, it **cannot assume the manufacturer’s substantive responsibilities** (e.g., design, clinical evaluation, risk management). The AR’s **liability is triggered only if the manufacturer fails to comply with Article 10 obligations**, making it essential for the AR to **actively monitor the manufacturer’s compliance** and **terminate the mandate if necessary**.

> ⚠ **FAITHFULNESS FLAG** — 1 of 1 verbatim quote(s) could not be matched against the retrieved regulatory context and have been removed from the answer. Treat the underlying point as unverified until checked against the source provisions.
> - “**: The AR’s role is **procedural and verificatory**, not substantive. It ensures the manufacturer’s compliance but *...”

results.label: SaMD qualification — clinical decision support as a medical device (MDCG 2019-11)
results.reliance: Cannot be relied on without major revision
results.answer:
### **Direct AnswerYes**, standalone clinical decision-support software (CDSS) **qualifies as a medical device under the MDR 2017/745** if it meets the legal definition of a "medical deviceDecision Support Software [...] intended to provide healthcare professionals and/or users with recommendations for diagnosis, prognosis, monitoring and treatment of individual patients [...] are qualified as medical devices.simple searchSoftware intended to provide information which is used to take decisions with diagnosis or therapeutic purposes is classified as **class IIa**, except if such decisions have an impact that may cause:*
> - *death or an irreversible deterioration of a person's state of health, in which case it is in **class III**; or*
> - *a serious deterioration of a person's state of health or a surgical intervention, in which case it is classified as **class IIb**.The wording ‘intended to provide information which is used to take decisions with diagnosis or therapeutic purposes’ describes the ‘mode of action’ characteristic of all MDSW. Therefore, this sub-rule is generally applicable to all MDSW.MDSW intended to monitor physiological processes [...] where the nature of variations could result in immediate danger to the patient, is classified as class IIb.All other software is classified as class I.high-risk AI systemwellness" vs. "diagnosisConsideration of changes to an MDSW"**).

#### **When in Doubt**
- Consult **MDCG guidance** (e.g., **MDCG 2021-24** for classification principles).
- Seek **notified body opinion** for borderline cases.
- Request **Commission determination** under **Article 4 MDR** if regulatory status is disputed.

---

### **6. Interpretive Guidance (Non-Binding)**
#### **MDCG 2019-11 Key Takeaways**
1. **Qualification**:
 - Standalone CDSS is **MDSW** if it **processes data for a medical purpose** (e.g., diagnosis, treatment recommendations).
 - **Excluded**: Software for **general wellness, population analytics, or simple data storage**.
2. **Classification**:
 - **Class IIa**: Default for most CDSS (e.g., risk calculators).
 - **Class III**: If output could **cause death or irreversible harm** (e.g., AI-driven cancer diagnosis).

results.label: MDR vigilance — serious incident vs FSCA reporting (Article 87)
results.reliance: Can be used as a strong first draft
results.answer:
### **Manufacturer’s Vigilance Reporting Obligations under MDR 2017/745: Distinction and Timelines**

#### **1. Key Definitions (Article 2 MDR)**
- **Serious incident**: Any incident that directly or indirectly led, might have led, or might lead to:
  - Death of a patient, user, or other person,
  - Temporary or permanent serious deterioration of health,
  - A serious public health threat.
  *(Article 2(64))*

- **Field safety corrective action (FSCA)**: Corrective action taken by a manufacturer for **technical or medical reasons** to **prevent or reduce the risk of a serious incident** in relation to a device made available on the market.
  *(Article 2(65))*

- **Corrective action**: Action taken to eliminate the cause of a potential or actual non-conformity or other undesirable situation.
  *(Article 2(66))*

---

#### **2. Reporting Obligations (Article 87 MDR)**
Manufacturers **must report** to competent authorities via the electronic system (Article 92):
- **(a) Serious incidents** (except expected side-effects documented in technical documentation and subject to trend reporting under Article 88).
- **(b) Field safety corrective actions (FSCAs)**, including those undertaken in a **third country** if the device is also legally available in the EU and the reason for the FSCA is not limited to the third country.

---

#### **3. Distinction Between Serious Incidents and FSCAs**
| **Aspect**               | **Serious Incident**                          | **Field Safety Corrective Action (FSCA)**                     |
|--------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------|
| **Trigger**              | Actual or potential harm to health/safety.    | Preventive or corrective measure to mitigate risk of harm.    |
| **Reporting Scope**      | Individual events (unless periodic summary reporting applies). | Actions taken (e.g., recalls, software updates, warnings).     |
| **Timing**               | Immediate to 15 days (depending on severity).  | **In advance** of the action (unless urgent).                 |
| **Purpose**              | Alert authorities to risks.                   | Inform authorities of risk mitigation measures.               |

---

#### **4. Reporting Timelines for Serious Incidents (Article 87(3)-(5))**
The timeline depends on the **severity of the incident** and the **establishment of causality**:

| **Scenario**                                                                 | **Timeline**                                                                 |
|------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **General rule** (causal relationship established or reasonably possible)    | **Immediately** after establishing causality, **but no later than 15 days** after awareness. *(Article 87(3))* |
| **Serious public health threat**                                             | **Immediately**, **no later than 2 days** after awareness. *(Article 87(4))* |
| **Death or unanticipated serious deterioration in health**                   | **Immediately** after establishing/suspecting causality, **no later than 10 days** after awareness. *(Article 87(5))* |
| **Uncertainty about reportability**                                          | **Report within the applicable timeline** (e.g., 15 days) even if uncertain. *(Article 87(7))* |
| **Incomplete initial report**                                                | Submit an **initial report** followed by a **complete report** if needed. *(Article 87(6))* |

**Manufacturer awareness date**: The date when the first employee/representative of the manufacturer receives information about the incident (interpreted via MDCG guidance, though not legally binding).

---

#### **5. Reporting Timelines for Field Safety Corrective Actions (FSCAs) (Article 87(8))**
- **General rule**: Report **in advance** of the FSCA being undertaken, **without undue delay**.
- **Exception**: If the FSCA is **urgent** (e.g., immediate risk to health), the manufacturer may undertake the action **immediately** and report **as soon as possible thereafter**.

---

#### **6. Periodic Summary Reporting (Article 87(9))**
- **Applicability**: For **similar serious incidents** with the **same device or device type**, where:
  - The **root cause has been identified**, or
  - An FSCA has been implemented, or
  - The incidents are **common and well-documented**.
- **Condition**: The **coordinating competent authority** (Article 89(9)) must agree with the manufacturer on the **format, content, and frequency** of the periodic summary report.
- **Purpose**: Reduces administrative burden for recurring incidents with known causes.

---

#### **7. Investigation and Follow-Up (Article 89 MDR)**
- **Manufacturer’s obligation**: After reporting a serious incident, the manufacturer must:
  - Perform **necessary investigations** without delay, including a **risk assessment** of the incident and FSCA.
  - Provide a **final report** to the competent authority via the electronic system (Article 92), detailing:
    - Findings from the investigation,
    - Conclusions,
    - Corrective actions taken or planned.
  *(Article 89(1), (5))*

- **Competent authority’s role**:
  - Evaluates the **risks** and **adequacy of the FSCA**, considering factors such as:
    - Causality, detectability, probability of recurrence,
    - Frequency of device use,
    - Severity of harm,
    - Clinical benefit of the device,
    - Affected population.
  *(Article 89(3))*
  - May **intervene** in the manufacturer’s investigation or initiate an **independent investigation** if necessary.
  *(Article 89(4))*

---

#### **8. Field Safety Notices (Article 89(8))**
- **Obligation**: The manufacturer must **inform users** of the FSCA via a **field safety notice (FSN)**.
- **Language**: The FSN must be in an **official Union language** determined by the Member State where the FSCA is taken.
- **Draft submission**: Except in urgent cases, the **draft FSN** must be submitted to the **evaluating competent authority** (or coordinating competent authority for multi-country FSCAs) for comments before dissemination.

---

#### **9. Electronic Reporting System (Article 92 MDR)**
- All reports (serious incidents, FSCAs, trend reports, PSURs, FSNs) must be submitted via the **electronic system** (Eudamed’s Vigilance module).
- Reports are **automatically transmitted** to:
  - The competent authority of the **Member State where the incident occurred** (for serious incidents),
  - The competent authorities of **Member States where the FSCA is undertaken** and the **Member State of the manufacturer’s registered place of business** (for FSCAs),
  - The **notified body** that issued the certificate for the device (where applicable).

---

#### **10. Role of the Person Responsible for Regulatory Compliance (PRRC) (Article 15 MDR)**
- The PRRC is responsible for ensuring that:
  - The **reporting obligations under Articles 87–91** are fulfilled.
  *(Article 15(3)(d))*

---

### **Summary of Key Takeaways**
1. **Serious incidents** must be reported **immediately to 15 days** after awareness, depending on severity.
2. **FSCAs** must be reported **in advance** (unless urgent), with timelines tied to the action’s implementation.
3. **Investigations** are mandatory after reporting a serious incident, with a **final report** submitted to authorities.
4. **Field safety notices** must be issued to users, with drafts submitted to authorities for non-urgent cases.
5. **Periodic summary reporting** may be used for recurring incidents with known causes, subject to authority approval.
6. All reports are submitted via the **electronic system (Eudamed)** and shared with relevant authorities and notified bodies.

---
### **Regulatory Context References**
- **Serious incident reporting**: Article 87(1)(a), (3)-(7) MDR.
- **FSCA reporting**: Article 87(1)(b), (8) MDR.
- **Investigation obligations**: Article 89(1), (5) MDR.
- **Field safety notices**: Article 89(8) MDR.
- **Electronic reporting**: Article 92 MDR.
- **PRRC responsibilities**: Article 15(3)(d) MDR.

results.label: GDPR controller vs processor — SaMD cloud vendor (Articles 4, 24, 28)
results.reliance: Can be used as a strong first draft
results.answer:
### **Direct Answer**
The cloud vendor is **a processor** under the GDPR, provided it processes personal data **only on the hospital’s documented instructions** and does not determine the purposes or means of the processing. The hospital, as the entity deciding why and how the AI is used for diagnosis, remains the **controller**.

What determines this classification is the **formal definition of ‘controller’ and ‘processor’ in Article 4 GDPR**:
- The controller is the body that **determines the purposes and means** of processing.
- The processor is the body that **processes personal data on behalf of the controller**.

---

### **Supporting Analysis**

#### **1. Legal Definitions (Article 4 GDPR)**
- **Controller** [1] Article 4(7):
  ‘controller’ means … the body which, **alone or jointly with others, determines the purposes and means** of the processing of personal data.
- **Processor** [1] Article 4(8):
  ‘processor’ means … the body which **processes personal data on behalf of the controller**.

#### **2. Hospital as Controller**
The hospital decides:
- **Why** the AI is used (diagnostic purposes).
- **How** the AI is deployed (which patient data are fed into the model, what clinical decisions are informed by the output, etc.).
→ The hospital therefore **determines the purposes and means** and is the controller.

#### **3. Cloud Vendor as Processor**
The vendor:
- Hosts the AI model and runs inference **only on the hospital’s instructions**.
- Does **not** decide why the data are processed or what clinical decisions are taken.
- Must process the data **exclusively on documented instructions** from the hospital (Article 28(3)(a) GDPR).
→ The vendor therefore **processes on behalf of the controller** and is a processor.

#### **4. Key Determinant: Who Decides Purposes and Means?**
- If the vendor **deviates from the hospital’s instructions** (e.g., uses the data for its own model training or marketing), it **becomes a controller** for that processing (Article 28(10) GDPR).
- If the vendor **strictly follows the hospital’s documented instructions**, it remains a processor.

---

### **Conclusion**
The cloud vendor is a **processor** under the GDPR, and the hospital is the **controller**. The decisive factor is that the hospital determines the **purposes and means** of the processing, while the vendor merely executes those instructions.

results.label: GDPR Article 28 — mandatory content of a data processing agreement
results.reliance: Can be used as a strong first draft
results.answer:
### **Direct Answer**
Under **GDPR Article 28(3)**, when a **medical device manufacturer (controller)** engages a **processor**, the **data processing agreement (DPA)** must contain the following **mandatory clauses** (verbatim from the context):

---

### **1. Binding Legal Instrument**
The processing must be governed by a **contract or other legal act under Union or Member State law** that is **binding on the processor** with regard to the controller. This must set out:
- The **subject-matter** of the processing,
- The **duration** of the processing,
- The **nature and purpose** of the processing,
- The **type of personal data** processed,
- The **categories of data subjects**, and
- The **obligations and rights of the controller**.

[2] Article 28(3) GDPR: *"Processing by a processor shall be governed by a contract or other legal act under Union or Member State law, that is binding on the processor with regard to the controller and that sets out the subject-matter and duration of the processing, the nature and purpose of the processing, the type of personal data and categories of data subjects and the obligations and rights of the controller."*

---

### **2. Specific Processor Obligations**
The contract must **stipulate, in particular**, that the processor:
#### **a. Processes data only on documented instructions**
- The processor must process personal data **only on documented instructions from the controller**, including for **transfers to third countries or international organisations**, unless required by Union/Member State law (in which case, the processor must inform the controller before processing, unless prohibited by law).

[2] Article 28(3)(a) GDPR: *"[The processor shall] process the personal data only on documented instructions from the controller, including with regard to transfers of personal data to a third country or an international organisation, unless required to do so by Union or Member State law to which the processor is subject; in such a case, the processor shall inform the controller of that legal requirement before processing, unless that law prohibits such information on important grounds of public interest."*

#### **b. Ensures confidentiality**
- Persons authorised to process personal data must be **under a statutory obligation of confidentiality** or have **committed themselves to confidentiality**.

[2] Article 28(3)(b) GDPR: *"[The processor shall] ensure that persons authorised to process the personal data have committed themselves to confidentiality or are under an appropriate statutory obligation of confidentiality."*

#### **c. Implements appropriate security measures**
- The processor must take **all measures required under Article 32 GDPR** (e.g., pseudonymisation, encryption, resilience of systems).

[2] Article 28(3)(c) GDPR: 

#### **d. Engages sub-processors only with prior authorisation**
- The processor **cannot engage another processor without prior specific or general written authorisation** from the controller.
- If general authorisation is given, the processor must **inform the controller of any intended changes** (addition/replacement of sub-processors) and **allow the controller to object**.
- Sub-processors must be bound by the **same data protection obligations** as the original processor.

[2] Article 28(2) GDPR: *"The processor shall not engage another processor without prior specific or general written authorisation of the controller. In the case of general written authorisation, the processor shall inform the controller of any intended changes concerning the addition or replacement of other processors, thereby giving the controller the opportunity to object to such changes."*
[2] Article 28(4) GDPR: *"Where a processor engages another processor for carrying out specific processing activities on behalf of the controller, the same data protection obligations as set out in the contract or other legal act between the controller and the processor [...] shall be imposed on that other processor by way of a contract or other legal act under Union or Member State law."*

#### **e. Assists the controller with data subject rights**
- The processor must **assist the controller** (via technical/organisational measures) in responding to requests for exercising data subject rights (e.g., access, rectification, erasure, restriction) **to the extent possible**.

[2] Article 28(3)(e) GDPR: 

#### **f. Assists the controller with GDPR compliance**
- The processor must assist the controller in ensuring compliance with **Articles 32–36 GDPR** (security, data breach notification, DPIAs, prior consultation with supervisory authorities).

[2] Article 28(3)(f) GDPR: *"[The processor shall] assist the controller in ensuring compliance with the obligations pursuant to Articles 32 to 36 taking into account the nature of processing and the information available to the processor."*

#### **g. Deletes or returns data after processing ends**
- At the controller’s choice, the processor must **delete or return all personal data** after the end of the processing services, unless Union/Member State law requires storage.

results.answer:
### **Direct Answer**
To lawfully process patients’ health data for training and running a clinical AI system under the **GDPR**, you must satisfy **two cumulative legal bases**:
1. **A general lawful basis under Article 6(1)** (e.g., consent, contract, legal obligation, public interest, or legitimate interest), **and**
2. **A derogation under Article 9(2)** for processing **special categories of personal data** (health data).

The most relevant derogations for clinical AI are:
- **Article 9(2)(h)** – processing for **healthcare or medical diagnosis** (if the AI is used in direct patient care), **or**
- **Article 9(2)(i)** – processing for **public health purposes** (e.g., population-level analytics, disease surveillance, or improving healthcare systems).

If the AI system is **not directly involved in patient care** (e.g., purely for research or development), **explicit consent (Article 9(2)(a))** may be required unless another derogation applies (e.g., **Article 9(2)(j)** for scientific research under safeguards).

---

### **1. Actor Status (GDPR)**
The entity processing health data is a **data controller** (Article 4(7) GDPR) if it determines the **purposes and means** of processing. If the AI system is developed or deployed by a healthcare provider, hospital, or medical device manufacturer, that entity is typically the **controller**.

---

### **2. Classification Route (GDPR)**
- **Health data** = **special category of personal data** (Article 9(1) GDPR, defined in Article 4(15) as "data concerning health").
- **Processing health data is prohibited by default** (Article 9(1)) unless a **derogation under Article 9(2)** applies.
- **Dual-basis rule**: Even if a derogation under Article 9(2) applies, a **lawful basis under Article 6(1)** is still required.

---

### **3. Trigger Event (GDPR)**
The obligation to establish a lawful basis is triggered when:
- The controller **collects, stores, or uses** health data (e.g., for training AI models or running clinical AI systems).
- The processing involves **special categories of data** (health data).

---

### **4. Obligation Clusters (GDPR)**
#### **A. Lawful Basis (Article 6(1))**
The controller must identify **one of the following** (most relevant for clinical AI):
- **(a) Consent** – Freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous (Article 4(11)).
  - *Note*: Consent is often **not ideal** for clinical AI due to power imbalances in healthcare and the difficulty of withdrawing consent retroactively.
- **(c) Legal obligation** – If processing is required by EU/Member State law (e.g., public health mandates).
- **(d) Vital interests** – Only if necessary to protect someone’s life (rarely applicable for AI training).
- **(e) Public interest** – If processing is necessary for a task carried out in the public interest (e.g., improving healthcare systems, disease surveillance).
- **(f) Legitimate interest** – Only if the controller’s interests **override** the data subject’s rights (unlikely for health data unless pseudonymized and low-risk).

#### **B. Derogation for Special Categories (Article 9(2))**
The controller must also satisfy **one of the following** (most relevant for clinical AI):
- **(a) Explicit consent** – Must be **specific, informed, and freely given** (higher standard than Article 6(1)(a)).
  - *Note*: Explicit consent is **not always feasible** in healthcare due to power dynamics and the need for ongoing data use.
- **(h) Healthcare or medical diagnosis** – Processing is necessary for:
  - **Preventive or occupational medicine**,
  - **Medical diagnosis**,
  - **Provision of health/social care**, or
  - **Management of health/social care systems**,
  - *and* the data is processed by (or under the responsibility of) a **healthcare professional** subject to **confidentiality obligations** (Article 9(3)).
- **(i) Public health** – Processing is necessary for **public health reasons** (e.g., disease surveillance, improving healthcare quality) **based on EU/Member State law** that provides **suitable safeguards** (e.g., pseudonymization, access controls).
- **(j) Scientific research** – If processing is necessary for **scientific research** and:
  - The research is in the **public interest**,
  - **Appropriate safeguards** are in place (e.g., pseudonymization, data minimization),
  - The research **cannot be achieved without the data**,
  - *and* the processing is **proportionate** to the aim pursued.

#### **C. Additional Obligations**
- **Transparency (Articles 13–14)** – Inform data subjects about:
  - The **purpose** of processing,
  - The **legal basis** (Article 6 + Article 9 derogation),
  - **Data retention periods**,
  - **Rights** (e.g., access, rectification, erasure, objection),
  - **Automated decision-making** (if applicable, Article 22).
- **Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) (Article 35)** – **Mandatory** if processing involves:
  - **Large-scale processing of health data** (Article 35(3)(b)), **or**
  - **Automated decision-making with legal/significant effects** (Article 35(3)(a)).
- **Security (Article 32)** – Implement **appropriate technical/organizational measures** (e.g., encryption, access controls).
- **Data Minimization (Article 5(1)(c))** – Only process data **necessary** for the purpose.
- **Storage Limitation (Article 5(1)(e))** – Retain data only as long as necessary.

---

### **5. Cross-Regulatory Dependencies**
#### **A. Interaction with MDR/IVDR (if the AI system is a medical device)**
- If the AI system is a **medical device** (MDR) or **in vitro diagnostic device** (IVDR), compliance with **MDR/IVDR** does **not replace GDPR obligations**.
- **MDR/IVDR** require:
  - **Clinical evaluation** (MDR Annex XIV),
  - **Risk management** (ISO 14971),
  - **Data protection by design** (MDR Article 10(2)),
  - **Post-market surveillance** (MDR Article 83).
- **GDPR** applies **independently** – even if the AI system complies with MDR/IVDR, a **lawful basis under GDPR** is still required.

#### **B. Interaction with EU AI Act (if the AI system is high-risk)**
- If the AI system is **high-risk under the AI Act** (e.g., Annex III, point 1 – medical devices), the **AI Act imposes additional requirements**:
  - **Risk management system** (Article 9),
  - **Data governance** (Article 10),
  - **Transparency** (Article 13),
  - **Human oversight** (Article 14),
  - **Accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity** (Article 15).
- **AI Act does not replace GDPR** – both must be complied with **independently**.
- **AI Act Article 10(5)** explicitly states that **training data must comply with GDPR**.

---

### **6. Residual Uncertainty**
- **Case-by-case assessment required**:
  - Whether **Article 9(2)(h) (healthcare)** or **9(2)(i) (public health)** applies depends on the **specific use case** (e.g., direct patient care vs. population-level analytics).
  - **Explicit consent (Article 9(2)(a))** may be required if no other derogation applies (e.g., for research not covered by public interest).
- **Member State variations**:
  - Some Member States have **additional national laws** under Article 9(4) GDPR that may restrict or expand derogations.
- **DPIA triggers**:
  - If the AI system involves **profiling or automated decision-making**, a **DPIA is mandatory** (Article 35(3)(a)).
  - If processing is **large-scale**, a **DPIA is mandatory** (Article 35(3)(b)).

---

### **7. Interpretive Guidance (Non-Binding)**
**MDCG 2025-6** (non-binding guidance on MDR/IVDR and AI Act interplay) states:
- **Transparency and explainability** are key for clinical AI, aligning with **GDPR’s right to explanation** (Recital 71).
- **Data processing for AI training** must comply with **GDPR’s principles** (e.g., purpose limitation, data minimization).
- **Health institutions** using **in-house AI** must still comply with **GDPR**, even if exempt from MDR/IVDR third-party conformity assessment.

**Key takeaway**: While MDCG guidance is **non-binding**, it reinforces that **GDPR compliance is a prerequisite** for lawful AI deployment in healthcare.

---

### **Conclusion: Most Likely Lawful Bases for Clinical AI**
| **Scenario** | **Article 6(1) Basis** | **Article 9(2) Derogation** | **Key Conditions** |
|-------------|----------------------|----------------------------|-------------------|
| **AI for direct patient care** (e.g., diagnostic support) | Public interest (6(1)(e)) or Legal obligation (6(1)(c)) | 9(2)(h) (healthcare) | Processed by/under healthcare professional; confidentiality safeguards |
| **AI for public health** (e.g., disease surveillance) | Public interest (6(1)(e)) | 9(2)(i) (public health) | Based on EU/Member State law; suitable safeguards |
| **AI for research** (e.g., training models) | Public interest (6(1)(e)) or Legitimate interest (6(1)(f)) (if low-risk) | 9(2)(j) (scientific research) | Public interest; pseudonymization; cannot be achieved otherwise |
| **AI for non-care purposes** (e.g., hospital admin) | Contract (6(1)(b)) or Legitimate interest (6(1)(f)) | Explicit consent (9(2)(a)) | Freely given, specific, informed; must be withdrawable |

**Recommendation**:
- **For clinical AI in patient care**: Use **Article 6(1)(e) (public interest) + Article 9(2)(h) (healthcare)**.
- **For public health AI**: Use **Article 6(1)(e) (public interest) + Article 9(2)(i) (public health)**.
- **For research AI**: Use **Article 6(1)(e) (public interest) + Article 9(2)(j) (scientific research)** with **pseudonymization and DPIA**.
- **Avoid consent where possible** due to practical challenges in healthcare settings.
